w:r.w:t: This document translates the architectural analysis of the outfit stylist pipeline into concrete, file-by-file implementation instructions. Each change is scoped, ordered by priority, and includes the exact code patterns to use. Nothing here requires a new ML model — all improvements leverage infrastructure already present in your codebase.
Before touching code, understand exactly why results are poor. There are four independent failure modes, each fixable without the others.
Failure 1 — Retrieval searches the wrong space

What happens now: you compute a centroid of anchor item embeddings and run kNN from that centroid. The centroid vector points toward things similar to your anchors — more tops if you have tops. You are finding neighbors of the anchor, not complements to it.
What should happen: a navy blazer + white shirt should retrieve trousers, shoes, and belts — not more blazers. This requires searching in the complementary embedding space, not the similarity space.
This single bug explains the majority of irrelevant recommendations. Fix this first.
Failure 2 — Stylist signals are metadata-derived, not vision-derived

Your aesthetic, styleTokens, and colorHarmony fields are computed from title and description text fields. A vendor tagging a product 'casual chic' gives you nothing reliable. The CLIP-derived embedding_color, embedding_style, and embedding_pattern fields — which would be reliable — are missing on older catalog documents. Per the implementation status docs, backfill has not been run.
Your scoring model is grading on phantom signals for a large fraction of catalog items. Check what percentage of retrieval candidates have null embedding_color — it is likely above 40%.
Failure 3 — No occasion or context signal

The pipeline has no concept of why the user is building an outfit. The static rules (dress → shoes essential) are occasion-blind. The same anchor blazer should produce completely different completions for 'office Monday' vs 'dinner date' vs 'weekend brunch'. There is no mechanism to thread this context through retrieval or scoring.
Failure 4 — Outfit sets have scores but no story

A user sees a set of items with a coherenceScore of 0.74 but has no idea why these pieces work together or whether to trust it. The absence of a human-readable outfit narrative kills user trust in recommendations that may actually be correct.
These five changes fix the most critical failures. None require model training. All are additive to existing code.
Until this runs, embedding_color / embedding_style / embedding_pattern are null on older products, meaning your multi-signal scoring silently degrades to near zero for a large portion of candidates.
Step 2 — Add a monitoring check to your health endpoint
Add this function and call it from your readiness check:
export async function checkEmbeddingCoverage(): Promise<{ ok: boolean; coverage: number }> {
Target: coverage > 0.85 before enabling multi-signal scoring in production. Log a warning if below threshold.
Over-aggressive slot contamination rejection silently returns fewer results than requested without surfacing an error. Users get 2 suggestions when 8 were expected.
Find the slot contamination rejection block (the hard guard before scoring acceptance). Add explicit logging so you can measure the rejection rate:
At the end of retrieval, add:
Then run the regression matrix to see which slot combinations are failing:
For any combination with < 3 suggestions returned, reduce the acceptance floor by 0.05 for that slot specifically and re-test.
2.3 Add occasion as a first-class input field
The single change with the largest perceptible quality jump. Occasion-awareness makes every recommendation feel intentional rather than generic.
// Add to the complete-look request body parsing:
Add this utility object near the top of recommendations.service.ts:
const OCCASION_FORMALITY: Record<string, { min: number; max: number; avoidSlots: string[] }> = {
Step 4 — Apply formality gates in candidate scoring
In the scoring function, before applying formalityAlignment weight, gate on occasion:
// Hard reject: outside formality band for this occasion
Update the API docs and the wardrobe complete-look section in api-reference.md to document the new occasion field.
2.4 Fix centroid retrieval — switch to complementary-space search
This is the root cause of 'more of the same' recommendations. The centroid of two tops embeddings retrieves more tops, not bottoms.

For each slot pair (e.g. tops → bottoms), you need a vector that points from the anchor space toward the complement space. You can bootstrap this from co-occurrence in your existing data without any training.
Step 1 — Create the delta vector builder script
async function buildDelta(fromSlot: string, toSlot: string): Promise<number[]> {
// Fetch products from each slot that appear together in outfit sets
SELECT p1.primary_image_id as from_img, p2.primary_image_id as to_img
JOIN outfit_items oi2 ON oi1.outfit_id = oi2.outfit_id AND oi1.id != oi2.id
JOIN products p1 ON oi1.product_id = p1.id
JOIN products p2 ON oi2.product_id = p2.id
WHERE p1.category ILIKE $1 AND p2.category ILIKE $2
// Fetch embeddings from OpenSearch for each image ID
return subtractVectors(toMean, fromMean); // The complementary offset
logger.warn('slot_deltas_not_found — falling back to centroid search');
Step 3 — Apply delta in candidate retrieval
In the kNN query construction block, replace the plain centroid with delta-adjusted query:
return anchorEmbedding; // Fallback to similarity search
const shifted = anchorEmbedding.map((v, i) => v + delta[i] * 0.4);

The 0.4 scale factor on the delta is a starting point. It controls how strongly you push toward complementary space. Tune from 0.2 to 0.6 based on qualitative evaluation. Too high and results become too distant from the anchor style.
2.5 Add outfit narrative via Gemini (no training required)
A one-sentence styling reason transforms user trust. Users can tell if the narrative is plausible, which also acts as a quality gate — bad recommendations produce incoherent narratives you can catch.
anchors: Array<{ title: string; category: string; color?: string }>;
suggestions: Array<{ title: string; category: string; color?: string }>;
'Write exactly ONE sentence (max 25 words) explaining why these pieces work together.',
'Be specific about colors, silhouette, or occasion. Do not use filler phrases like\'great choice\'.',
'Respond with ONLY the one sentence. No preamble.'
return 'These pieces complement each other in color and formality.';
File: src/routes/wardrobe/recommendations.service.ts, after set scoring is complete:
// After keeping top 5 sets, enrich with narratives in parallel:
anchors: anchorItems.map(a => ({ title: a.name, category: a.category, color: a.color })),
return { title: item?.title ?? '', category: item?.category ?? '', color: item?.color };
Step 3 — Add narrative to response type
Update the outfitSets response to include the narrative field. Document it in api-reference.md.
Replaces unreliable text-derived aesthetic with vision-derived one using CLIP similarity to curated reference images. No training required — just reference image curation.

Create 20-30 reference images per aesthetic category. At index time, compute cosine similarity between each product's CLIP embedding and each aesthetic centroid. Store the winning aesthetic and confidence as aesthetic_clip in OpenSearch.
Step 1 — Create the aesthetic reference set
// These are the aesthetic categories your pipeline will classify into.
// For each, you need a centroid vector derived from reference images.
// Run scripts/build-aesthetic-centroids.ts once to generate and save these.
'minimalist', // clean lines, neutral colors, no print

Curate 20-30 product URLs per aesthetic from your catalog. Fetch their CLIP embeddings from OpenSearch, compute the mean vector per aesthetic, and save to models/aesthetic_centroids.json. This is a one-time operation.
Step 3 — Classify products at index time
// After computing main embedding, add aesthetic classification:
Step 4 — Update scoring to prefer aesthetic_clip over aesthetic
// Prefer vision-derived aesthetic when available and confident
You cannot improve a system you have no signal on. Every outfit approval/dismissal is training data. This endpoint costs almost nothing to build and everything to not have.
user_id BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
anchor_item_ids JSONB NOT NULL, -- wardrobe item IDs used as anchors
suggested_product_ids JSONB NOT NULL, -- the outfit set suggested
action TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (action IN ('save', 'dismiss', 'edit', 'click_item')),
edited_product_ids JSONB, -- what user changed it to, if edited
CREATE INDEX idx_outfit_feedback_created ON outfit_set_feedback(created_at DESC);
3.3 Add formality score to OpenSearch index
Formality alignment currently uses stored metadata which is sparse. This adds a computed formality score at index time from CLIP similarity to formality reference embeddings.
Add formality anchors — two text embeddings representing the formality poles:
// Pre-computed once at startup, cached in memory
formal: await getTextEmbedding('formal professional business attire suit blazer'),
casual: await getTextEmbedding('casual everyday relaxed comfortable streetwear'),
// In buildProductSearchDocument, after embedding is computed:
Add formality_clip: computeFormalityScore(embedding) to the indexed document. This gives you a reliable, vision-derived formality score independent of title text.
3.4 Add diversity constraint to outfit set selection
Without diversity enforcement, buildOutfitSets returns 5 nearly identical sets — same items shuffled. Users see no real choice.
File: src/routes/wardrobe/recommendations.service.ts, in the set selection block after scoring:
// After scoring all candidate sets, apply MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance)
// Always pick the highest-scoring set first
// Similarity to already-selected sets: Jaccard on productIds
const mmrScore = lambda * relevance - (1 - lambda) * maxSim;
if (mmrScore > bestScore) { bestScore = mmrScore; bestIdx = i; }
function jaccardSimilarity(a: Set<number>, b: Set<number>): number {
Replace the current top-5 selection in buildOutfitSets with: return selectDiverseSets(allScoredSets, 5, 0.6);
4.1 Train a reranker on outfit feedback data
After collecting 400+ rows in outfit_set_feedback (2-3 weeks of traffic), train a small XGBoost reranker to replace the hardcoded weight blend in rerankCompleteLookFashionAware.
How many anchor items were used
One-hot: casual, work, formal, date, sport, travel
Label: action == 'save' or action == 'click_item' → positive (1.0), action == 'dismiss' → negative (0.0), action == 'edit' → 0.5.

Use the existing XGBoost training script at scripts/train_xgboost_ranker.py as a template. Save to models/outfit_reranker_model.json. Load in recommendations.service.ts alongside the existing recommendation ranker.
4.2 Add a Gemini-powered occasion enrichment step
When occasion is not passed by the client, infer it from the anchor items before running slot inference. This is the LLM layer that makes the pipeline context-aware.
anchors: Array<{ title: string; category: string; color?: string; styleTokens?: string[] }>
): Promise<{ occasion: string; confidence: number; reasoning: string }> {
'Based on these wardrobe items, infer the most likely occasion.',
Call this in runCompleteLookCore() when occasion is not provided by the client:
4.3 Add attribution tags to each suggestion
Users trust recommendations more when they know why a specific piece was selected. Add a machine-readable attribution that drives UI labels.
After per-candidate scoring, add attribution based on which signal drove the selection:
Add attribution: buildAttribution(fitBreakdown) to each suggestion in the response. Frontend can use this to show labels like 'Matches your color palette' or 'Complements the formality level'.
You cannot tune what you cannot measure. Add these Prometheus metrics alongside the existing ones in src/lib/metrics/.
Fraction rejected by slot contamination guard
Fraction of candidates with embedding_color populated
Gemini call latency for narrative generation
Track outfit_feedback_save_rate as your primary quality KPI. Target above 0.40 before considering the pipeline production-grade.
Manual smoke test: occasion=work vs occasion=date
Query tops anchor, assert bottoms in top results
Integration test: call complete-look, check narrative field
Compare aesthetic_clip vs aesthetic on 50 products
Assert top-5 sets share < 2 products across any two sets
Check formality_clip for known formal/casual items
Ship in this exact order to avoid regressions and to unblock later changes:
Run embedding backfill (2.1). Everything that uses embedding_color depends on this.
Add observability metrics (4.4). You need visibility before changing behavior.
Add slot rejection logging (2.2). Establishes a baseline before you change thresholds.
Run slot regression matrix. Fix any combination returning 0 results.
Add occasion field (2.3). This is additive — existing calls without occasion are unaffected.
Add outfit narrative (2.5). Additive, no impact on scoring.
Add feedback endpoint (3.2). Start collecting signal immediately.
Add diversity constraint (3.4). Test that sets no longer overlap.
Ship delta vector retrieval (2.4). This changes retrieval behavior — monitor suggestion count metrics closely for 48 hours.
Ship CLIP aesthetic classifier (3.1) and formality score (3.3). These require a partial reindex.
Ship occasion inference (4.2). Requires Gemini call per request — monitor latency.
Train outfit reranker (4.1) once feedback data is sufficient.
After shipping change 9 (delta vector retrieval), watch outfit_suggestions_returned_p50 for 48 hours. If median drops below 4, the delta scale factor (0.4) is too high — reduce to 0.2.
AI Outfit Stylist — Production Upgrade Plan
